claims: MemRef operations expose shape/layout/stride metadata and view-like reshapes/subviews/transposes without copying data when compatibility/contiguity conditions hold.
MemRef has explicit memory space casts, allocation/deallocation, stack allocation, metadata extraction, and lowering details relevant to LLVM GEP inbounds/nuw emission.
memref.distinct_objects lets optimizers assume listed memrefs do not mutually alias; if they do alias, load/store behavior is undefined.
Out-of-bounds subviews, non-contiguous collapse where unsupported, mismatched copy shapes, using an old memref after realloc, and invalid bounds can be UB.
caveats: Many operations are metadata transformations rather than full source-language semantics. UB consequences require frontend/verifier obligations before lowering from a source language.

claims: Linalg structured ops keep iteration spaces tied to input/output operands and indexing maps, making data/control relationships explicit.
Explicit iterator kinds such as parallel and reduction guide legality of transformations.
The dialect is intended to support buffer allocation, tiling, promotion to fast memory, tile-and-fuse, loop/hardware mapping, vectorization, and lowering to loops or library calls.
Views/strided memrefs preserve layout metadata and can expose subview/transpose-like layout transformations without data movement.
caveats: Docs describe design rationale and evolving implementation; frontend must ensure region behavior and iterator semantics are sound.

claims: Affine dialect is optimizer-oriented and uses restricted affine maps/loop forms to make dependence analysis and loop transformations efficient and reliable.
affine.for has affine-map bounds and positive constant strides; affine.load/store indices are affine expressions of loop IVs and symbols.
affine.parallel models hyper-rectangular affine parallel bands with reductions whose order is unspecified.
Affine prefetch, vector affine load/store, DMA, linearization/delinearization encode memory and locality facts in structured form.
caveats: The benefits depend on affine restrictions; semi-affine extensions may not be accepted by analyses expecting strict affine form. Some operations carry UB conditions.

claims: MLIR vector dialect represents n-dimensional vectors as optimizer-facing SSA values useful for transformations.
The dialect separates vector-value optimization from buffer/loop analysis and supports progressive lowering from virtual vectors to hardware vectors to LLVM.
Vector transfer operations encode memory-to-vector movement with permutation maps, allowing reasoning above scalar load/store form.
LLVM lowering must eventually fit LLVM native 1-D vectors or nested aggregates, with constraints for dynamic indexing and scalable dimensions.
caveats: Docs describe infrastructure rather than universal policy; target-friendly shapes and legal lowering remain constraints.

claims: LLVM has loop and SLP vectorizers enabled by default; loop vectorization widens iterations, while SLP combines independent scalar operations.
Loop Vectorizer uses a cost model for vector width and interleave factor; pragmas/hints can influence but not guarantee vectorization.
If pointer disjointness is not known statically, the loop vectorizer may add runtime pointer-overlap checks and fall back to scalar code if arrays may overlap.
The Loop Vectorizer handles reductions, inductions, unknown trip counts, epilogues, and some if-conversion; floating reductions generally need permissive math flags or ordered reductions.
caveats: Capability is not guarantee; profitability, legality, target support, alias/dependence information, and diagnostics matter.

claims: Halide separates algorithm specification from scheduling/optimization choices.
Schedules expose loop order, split, fuse, tiling, vectorization, unrolling, and parallelization choices.
Tiling can improve locality when neighboring outputs share overlapping input data; vectorization maps fixed inner ranges to vector computations; fusing tile loops can expose parallel tile execution.
Halide can print generated loop nests, making optimization choices explicit.
caveats: Examples are simple and benefits are conditional. Pure Funcs tolerate duplicate evaluation; external callbacks/update definitions impose correctness constraints.

claims: ISPC lets programmers write SPMD code where multiple program instances map to SIMD lanes in a gang.
uniform versus varying types expose whether values are shared across the gang or lane-varying, affecting scalarization and control flow.
foreach maps multiple logical indices to target-width lane-wise work; gang/mask size and target ISA are explicit compilation choices.
Control-flow divergence is represented with a per-instance execution mask; coherent branches improve SIMD utilization.
ISPC supports layout/performance controls such as soa<n>, alignment behavior, gathers/scatters, FMA, fast math, and address-width choices.
caveats: Some extracted claims came from visible guide excerpts; full rules need deeper source pass. Performance implications are target-dependent.

claims: Futhark arrays are regular/rectangular, and array sizes participate in types, reflecting efficient-compilation concerns.
SOACs and attributes guide parallelization/fusion; attributes can be ambiguous after fusion if conflicting.
In-place updates are semantically pure but can avoid copying by consuming the old array and requiring aliases to be unused afterward.
Consuming parameters use * to transfer ownership; consuming functions are restricted in higher-order contexts.
Alias analysis tracks simple bindings and components conservatively; size coercion can be runtime-checked.
caveats: Parallel constructs are not guaranteed to execute in parallel. The language reference is semantic; exact layout/codegen details need implementation docs/papers.

claims: Chapel arrays are defined over domains, first-class index-set descriptors; rectangular arrays use bounded integer ranges per dimension.
Named domains can let multiple arrays share an index set, reduce overhead for matching distributed arrays, and help compiler optimization.
Declaring invariant domains as const can enable key compiler optimizations.
forall can iterate over arrays/domains in parallel; whole-array assignment requires compatible shape rather than identical indices.
caveats: Primer does not enumerate specific optimizations or distribution mechanics. Aliasing/materialization details for slicing are not fully described.

claims: Fortran DO CONCURRENT models iterations as executable in arbitrary order, not necessarily actually parallel.
Restrictions constrain cross-iteration data flow and disallow constructs that prevent full iteration execution or introduce problematic effects.
Compilers may localize/privatize temporaries assigned before use in each iteration; post-loop values may be unusable.
Fortran 2018 locality specifiers LOCAL, SHARED, LOCAL_INIT, and DEFAULT(NONE) can communicate intent.
Implicit localization and safe parallel lowering are difficult when arrays, pointers, components, pure calls that read globals, or interprocedural effects obscure dependences.
caveats: Restrictions are necessary but not sufficient for automatic parallelization. Locality specifiers name variables, not arbitrary elements/slices/components.
